title: Iran Book Publishing History: Titles Published and Print-Run/Circulation, 1930s-2019
source_org: Multiple: Publishing Research Quarterly (Springer, peer-reviewed academic), Human Rights Activists / peace-mark.org (independent archive translating Persian journalism), Tehran Times (Iran state media, labeled)
time_coverage: 1931-2019 (irregular: individual years as reported by sources, not a continuous annual series)
units: titles published per year (count); average copies printed per title (circulation); total copies published (count); minutes per day (reading time survey)
license_terms: Academic article (Springer) fair-use excerpting of factual data points; independent archive and labeled state-media factual excerpting, per docs/bookkeeping.md source-reliability rules
notes: Complements (does not duplicate) the existing data/raw/iran-media-history/iranica-press-newspapers/press_periodicals_series.csv (which covers newspaper/periodical COUNTS 1898-1988, not books). This dataset is specifically about BOOKS: number of distinct titles published (a proxy for publishing-sector output) AND, separately and more interestingly for consumption purposes, average CIRCULATION per title (copies printed) which the peace-mark.org source argues is a better read-ership/demand proxy than title count alone -- title counts roughly doubled the 1980s while total copies printed fell by ~30 million and average circulation collapsed from historical norms of several thousand copies/title toward ~500-2000 by the 2010s (per multiple publisher interviews quoted in the peace-mark.org piece, not all individually tabulated here as they are qualitative anecdotes rather than a stated statistic). The 2018 figure (102,691 titles) is a secondary web-search compilation citing Iran Book House statistics, not independently cross-checked against a primary Khaneh Ketab table -- flagged as lower-confidence than the other rows. Springer article full text was NOT paywalled despite being hosted on link.springer.com/idp-authorize-redirecting infrastructure -- fetched successfully via direct curl with a browser user-agent; worth remembering for future Springer-hosted academic sources in this project.
